segs.utf8:
Prior to my NDE [Near-Death Experience], 
I was a real estate agent.
I was very focused on work and career.
I was happy, I would say.

But looking back now,
I see that my life was missing something.

It was still lonely
and a little bit empty.
My NDE was on August 22nd, 2009.
I was 34 years old.

I woke up with a tremendous amount
of pain, excruciating pain.

I was burning up
with a fever of 106 degrees F [41° C],

and we didn't know
what was going on.

A family member took me to the hospital
and they were running tests

and trying to figure out
what was happening.
I remember when I was in for my CT scan.
They were trying to adjust my I.V..
I could feel myself detaching.
I could feel myself coming out.
I would drift out almost the way

you see a balloon
start to drift away into the sky.
But that pain kept pulling me back in.

After my CT scan,
they brought me out into an area

in the emergency room where we were
just waiting for what was next.

And that's
when everything started happening,
when I really started dying, at that point.

I started convulsing and I could feel
the veins on my head popping out.

The pressure in my head
and in my body was tremendous.
The pain was excruciating.
My fever was high, so I was burning up.

But I was also freezing
cold all at the same time.

I remember convulsing
with my eyes closed,

and I had the thought of, I don't know
how much more of this I can take.

I don't think
I can take any more of this pain.

It was at that moment
that I completely disconnected

from everything that was external
and outside of me.

The people that were around me,
the medical staff,

the machines that I was hooked up to at that point –
I had no connection to it.

I lost the knowing that it was even there
and I was still experiencing

some of the pain,
but not to the same degree as I had been.

And I started doing an internal
scan of my body.
It felt like I was watching a movie.
I could see the blood pumping.
I could see my cells.
I could see my internal organs.
And it felt very factual.

I didn't really have any emotion
attached to it.

Then I had that awareness of
I'm dying.
This is what it feels like.

At that moment
I popped out of my body.

The next thing I knew,
I was on the ceiling,

looking down at myself
and everyone around me.

And I could see that there was no life
left in my body.
I knew that that was me, but
I didn't see me there anymore.

My soul was no longer
a part of that shell of a body
that I saw on the hospital gurney.
It was very real.

I knew that it was happening and I knew
that it was happening in that moment.
Everyone and everything
was very clear and in focus.

But it did feel like I was a little bit
detached from it.
As I was taking all of that in,
I started to very gently, very slowly
start floating up and backwards.

Everything that I was looking at
started to become smaller.

I felt that I was looking down
at the hospital

and I saw these little balls of energy
buzzing all around.
Those were souls that I was seeing,
and they were all connected by this
very thin, iridescent line,

the way that a spider web looks, if you
catch it just right in the sunlight.
That's what it looks like,

which I now realize is oneness
and the way that we are all connected.
And I remember the way that

these souls were buzzing around,
didn't make sense to me

because I thought,
why are they all running around like that?

I didn't understand
why they were all so busy.

And as I continued to float backwards,
I was moving a little faster at this point.
It was happening very quickly now,
where I felt like I was in this void
or in outer space.
It didn't feel scary, but it was black.

Then I immediately felt myself
go into the tunnel.

It was very black,
very dark in the tunnel.
I wasn't scared at all.
I was moving very fast.
It was exciting and it felt exhilarating.
The tunnel ride was very quick
and I popped right out of the tunnel
and I was immediately in the white light.
The light was so

bright and beautiful and powerful
that it took me a minute

to adjust and understand
what was going on there, too.
As soon as I started to settle into it
I understood that it was heaven.
I started to feel the light more
and feel the energy of what that was.
I immediately felt this overwhelming
sense of unconditional love.
Love like I have never felt before,

no matter what I had been through in my life,
no matter what I had done.
It was just complete love for me.
I was bathing in it.

It was wrapped around me
and it made me feel valued,
appreciated and accepted.

The next thing I felt
was an overwhelming sense of peace,

that everything was okay
and that everything was right.
That all is well.
I was getting a healing.
I started to notice

all the areas in my soul
or in my energetic space

where there were wounds
or there were holes.

All of those areas
were starting to get filled in,
and they were getting filled in with love.
I was being replenished,
and it was the best feeling.

It just made me feel like
this is who I am.
This is who I'm supposed to be.
This is what my soul is all about.

As that was happening,
I was also getting the sense that
this white light that was all around me,
that was taking such loving care of me –
that I am that white light.
I'm not separate from it.

I just had the illusion
that I had always been separate from it.
So while I was a part of it,

I really sensed that separation
being healed as well.
I knew that I was dead.
I knew that my physical body

was no longer working
and that I was no longer a part of it.
And I was fine with it.
It didn't scare me.

It just felt like
this is how it's meant to be.
It almost started feeling like my life
was becoming a distant memory.

It felt so much like home
to be in that light

that there was no other place
I wanted to be at that point.
There's no place that feels as good
as that light feels.

At that point, I wasn't thinking a lot
about the people that I had left behind.
I wonder if I had stayed longer,
if that would have come into play.

At the time of my NDE [Near-Death Experience],
I wasn't a mom yet and I'm a mom now.

I wonder a lot
how that would feel different
to have a child that I had left behind.

There’s just a sense
that everyone's going to be okay.

Everything's happening the way that it's
supposed to be happening,
and we're all taken care of,

and that it's all perfect
the way that it's happening.
Even when I say that now,

now that I'm back in a body
and I'm back in this physical world,
when I say, “I knew all was well,”

there's still that part of me
that goes, “What?”

It doesn't make sense to me
as a human being now.
But when we're out of our bodies,
you have a 360 degree perspective.
You're not limited by your body.
You're not limited by your brain.

You're not limited by your ego
because all of that is stripped away.

And when you do have that broad
360 degree perspective
feeling that “all is well” makes sense.

You understand that everything
happens for a reason.

Everything is happening
exactly as it should be.

And there's a lot of peace
that goes along with that.
That need to control
completely disappears.

You just allow everything
to be what it is.

After I was bathing
in the light for awhile

and really soaking that up
and starting to feel really good,
I did start to notice what I think
were other souls coming in around me.
It felt like a crowd coming toward me.
It felt like a friendly crowd.

I wasn't scared or intimidated,
but I didn't have enough time
to understand exactly what that was.

Because at that moment
I heard that inaudible voice
say to me, You need to go back.
And that's not what I wanted to hear.
No, no, no. I don't want to go.

Please don't make me go back
because it was so beautiful there.
Then I heard the voice again.
I get emotional about it even now.
I heard the voice again.
It said, You need to go back.
It was loving, but it was a firm voice

letting me know
that this is not a negotiation.
It was time to go back.
I persisted with the protesting.
I said, No, please don't make me go back.
And right at that moment, I felt

almost like I was nudged
back into the tunnel.

I went backwards
very fast into the tunnel,
very quickly back into my body.

I entered my body
through the top of my head.

It felt like I was being sucked
back into my body.
I felt the pain returning.
But it was very quick.
It was just enough for me to know
I'm back in, I'm back in this world.

Then I don't remember anything
for several hours after that.
When I woke up in pre-op [before surgery],

this was on a weekend
and it was very late at night.
I think it was at about nine or 10 p.m..
That part of the hospital was very empty.
I knew that I had died.
I knew that I had gone into this beautiful
white light that loved me.

But when I opened my eyes
I was receiving last rites,
and I thought, This is odd.

It didn't make sense to me,
and I was also trying to understand
what was going on around me.

I was trying to figure out
what the physical world was about

because it was almost like
I had lost the memory

of what these machines were
and what all these people were doing.

But I wasn't able to communicate
verbally at that point.
But as I was receiving last rites,
I had all these people around me.

The way people look at you
when they think you're going to die,

I've never been looked at
like that before in my life.
Family members and my doctor,

my surgeon, I remember especially the look
that they get in their eyes.

I've never seen that before and I remember
feeling their pain and concern.

I wanted to say,
What are you guys worried about?
If I was supposed to die
I would’ve been dead before,
I wouldn't have been sent back.

But I wasn't able to communicate
that at that time.
The next morning, when I woke up

my fever had been 106 [41°C],
but it went down to 94 degrees [34°C],
which is very dangerous.

The doctor explained to me
that I had a kidney stone

that had become lodged in my ureter
and it was stuck.

So everything... all of the toxins
that were meant to filter out
were going back into my bloodstream.
My entire blood supply had become infected
and I had gone into septic shock.

They were saying
the next 24 hours were critical.

We needed to watch
what was going on with my internal organs

and again, I just
wanted to say,
Everything's fine,
I'm going to be fine.
I know that, or I wouldn't be back here.
I was still very sick, though.

I was still having a lot of physical pain
and a lot of physical problems.
But I was having this dance with myself

where I was kind of floating out
and then I would float back in.

It was almost like I was trying
to work my way back into my body fully.

And I was uncomfortable
with everything around me

that was man-made:
the machines that I was hooked up to,

the supplies that were
there in the hospital.
The only thing that felt okay to me

at that point
were the people that were around me.
I could identify with other souls.

I was very fortunate
to have a big window

in the hospital room that I was in
that had beautiful trees outside of it.

I spent a lot of time
just staring at those trees

because that was what
I was truly connecting with,

that nature is what felt the most
like that white light to me.

I was sick for about
three months after that.
I had surgeries that followed.

My adrenal system was really taxed
by everything it had been through,

and I was experiencing
extreme exhaustion because of that.

I was also still taking
a lot of pain medication,
and I had the effects of my NDE.

I was recalling what had happened
and I knew that it was very real.

I tried to talk to
a couple of people about it,

two people that 
I was very close to.
And they rejected. They rejected it.

I think it's because when your family
or someone that loves you,

watches you die,
that's very traumatic for them.

Then they see you recovering
and see you starting to feel better.

I don't think that they wanted
to hear about death

and they didn't want to hear 
about the white light.

Then I also made the mistake
of mentioning it to my surgeon,
and he laughed at me.

So I just kept quiet about it 
during that time,

and doubt
started to creep in.
I was on heavy duty pain medication

and I thought, Maybe 
I'm having these recollections

and these experiences
as a result of the pain medication.
Maybe once I'm off the pain pills,
life will just go back to normal again.
But as soon as I was fully recovered,

fully off the pain medication,
things became more intense.

The memory of the white light,
the memory of everything

that led up to going into the light,
became more intense.
What I now realize is pain medication

was clouding everything for me
and muting everything for me.
And as soon as I was off of all of that,
things started to become crystal clear.

The memory of what happened,
and how extraordinary it was,
really started to sink in.
I started noticing the morning after

my NDE that I had these
increased sensitivities.

People that were 
coming in and out of my room,
I was picking up on their thoughts.
I knew what they were thinking.

I could feel
what they were feeling.

In a way, I didn't know
if I was making that up

or if I really was picking up on these thoughts
and feelings of other people,
if I really was an empath.

I would have conversations 
with other people

and I would say something to them
and they would just look at me.
They’d go, How do you know that?

I started realizing the experience
that I was having

was different than what
the people around me were having.

But I was picking up on thoughts
that were accurate
because they didn't know how

I knew that that was
what was going on with them.
And that was a lot, a lot to navigate.
The hardest part was, and still is,

feeling what everybody else feels,
because it can be
very overwhelming at times.
I was having a harder time
going out, leaving the house.

It was too chaotic there,
energetically, for me.
I was becoming so overwhelmed
by everything that was going on around me

and I had no idea how to manage
the loudness of the world.

Everything felt so loud
and amplified to me
that I started staying home more and more.

I only left for work,
and that was when I absolutely had to.
But I was only 34 at that time
and I knew that I had a lot of years

ahead of me and I knew that I couldn't
spend that time at home.

I had to figure out
how to be a part of the world again.

I lost a lot of relationships
after my near-death experience.
I was a completely different person

after that happened,
and they didn't know who I was.
I didn't know who I was, at first.

And so, when you become
a completely different person,
it's really hard to relate to people.
Relationships start to change.

I had this really big extraordinary
thing that happened to me

and I realized that I couldn't
talk to the people that were in my life.
And that's very sad. That hurt.
We all drifted apart,
friends, family members.
It was very difficult, very complicated.

I needed to make a career change
at that point.
Selling real estate,

I see the significance
in it, helping people find a home
to raise their families in.

But the business world, I couldn't
stomach the business world anymore.
It was a little too harsh for me.
I was no longer competitive at all.

I went from being
a competitive personality

to someone who just wanted 
to collaborate and to love,
and there's not a lot of space for that
in the business world.
I knew I needed to make a career change.
I didn't know what it would be.
So I did some hospice work

as I was in the middle
of moving into healing work.

And hospice, that was a great
training ground for me
and it was also a great way for me
to get back into that white light.
Because when I was working with patients
that were especially pre-active

or actively getting ready
to transition out of their bodies,

that white light is all around them,
and light beings,

and that presence of love,
is so much a part
of what they're experiencing.
They all had different religious beliefs

and they were all different ages
with different backgrounds.
It's not uncommon for people

to have that fear
of letting go of their bodies.

They all have various
reasons for that.

Sometimes they're very attached
to the physical world.

Sometimes they may be worried
about some of the ways that they lived

their lives, and so they have fear
of what could be next for them.
But, no matter what,

as soon as they’re 24 hours out from passing,
they're all peaceful.

They're all beginning their transition
at that point,
and they're feeling that love.

They're in process
at that time and the peace really sets in.

I remember I worked
at an inpatient hospice facility

and I would always go at night
on the weekdays because it was very quiet then

and that's when I liked to go
into work the most.

But there was one morning, 
I think it was a Sunday morning,

where I got a call
from the director and she said,

Jane, is there any way 
that you can come in?

We've got a gentleman here
who hasn't had

a single family member or friend
come to visit him.

He's been alone this whole time
and we feel sorry for him.

Can you just come and sit with him
and hold some space for him?

She said, He's not conscious,
but I know that he'll feel you there.

I remember when I walked in the room,
I could feel that peace

I could feel that love,
I could feel that presence there for him.

We knew that he would probably pass
probably by the end of that day.
We knew it was very soon.

When I walked into the room
and I felt that presence,
I felt like I needed to stand back

because what I was feeling
and sensing around him,
I felt that if I moved in too closely

that I would almost be 
interfering with this

beautiful experience
that this man was having.
He didn't feel alone.
He could feel all of the love
around him.

I wasn't needed at that point,
and I watched for a little while

and then I felt like
it was my time to walk out of the room
and to let his soul have that moment.

Because he didn't have anybody 
coming to see him.

So he must have led some type of 
a lonely or isolated life.

And there's pain
that goes along with that.
But what I saw him experiencing at the end
was that
he was completely loved

and he's completely
welcome into that love.

That was one of the most
beautiful moments that I experienced
doing hospice work.

I needed to do my own healing work
and experience my own healing
before I was able to fully move into this.
I was still dealing so much
from the pain of isolating after my NDE.

And so I learned a lot
by going through that experience myself.
I started realizing that I was able
to move energy and that scared me at first.

I didn't know what it meant
and I didn't know how to do it.
I didn't know who to talk to about it.

A therapist that I was seeing,
I remember her saying to me,

she said, Jane, when you talk,
I can feel that white light.

I can feel the qualities
of that white light coming out of you.

She said, I bet you one day
you're going to be able to fill

a whole room
with the energy of that white light.
I was intimidated by that,

but I thought that would be amazing
if I could do that.

She said,
You need to start practicing.

And I thought, I don't know 
what that means, to start practicing.

But I just started playing 
around with it and,

you know, filling up a corner
with white light,

just channeling, really,
but feeling that come through me

and trying to fill up
a space energetically with it.

It took a lot of practice
and fine tuning,

but once I was able to do that,
I started to realize that I could do that

with my own energetic space
and with other people's energetic space.

I wanted to be
really responsible about it.
So I took classes with healers

that I really trusted, healers
that are very ethical.
Reiki was the first thing that I did.

I just learned how to use my body
and my nervous system

almost as a map for what
other people are experiencing,

then learning how to move out
what's not working anymore,

what's low vibration or what's harmful
or what is trauma,

how to process that and move it out
and then fill it back up with love.
That's what I got during my NDE
and it took me, I would say,

at least a few years
of really diligent practice

to start feeling okay
with doing that for other people.
So that's how it got started
and then it just evolved in ways

that I couldn't have dreamed up
if I had tried.
It was donation-only healing work.

People started telling me,
You need to start charging.
I was very resistant to that.

But they were saying that
because they were sharing with me

how this energy work
was making them feel better.

Maybe they had done years
and years of talk therapy.
But this was the missing piece.
This was what was lasting.

The energy healing that I got during
my near-death experience,
that was what really worked
and really changed and impacted my life.

The white light has the answers
to all of our questions.

Questions that we didn't even know 
that we had.
Once I got into that white light,

a lot of the questions that I had
didn't even seem to matter anymore.
But the answers were still there.
Everything just made sense.
And all of that love

that is a part of who we are
and where we came from.
That's the answer to everything.
Because love
is the best healer that there is.

So any way
that we can tap into that white light,

what we're doing
is we're tapping into love

and we're tapping into healing
and we're tapping into peace.
All of our worries just melt away there.
The intuitive and psychic part

of the healing work
that I do, it all goes hand in hand.
I couldn't do the energy work that I do

without having heightened
or sharpened intuition.

Because I'm really tapping
into the subtleties

of everybody's energetic space
and feeling into everyone's

blind spots,
seeing there what they don't see,

what is deeply unconscious
or subconscious for them.

That's why the intuition
is so important in my work.

Intuition has become
a very important part of my life too,
because that has helped me
live more from a heart centered space

and drown out the voice of ego
that led me astray so many times

and really caused a lot
of destruction in my life.
So step one

for tapping into your true self,
the wisdom of your true self

and your higher self, or your soul –
is to quiet everything else down.
We didn't come into this world with egos.

We came in as our true self,
but we had to develop the ego to survive.
Ego is always talking to us.
It's who we think we are.

There are all of these running scripts
and unconscious,

subconscious, reels, thoughts,
that are going on.

It's mental chatter
and a lot of it is on autopilot.

Once you can start listening to that,
you'll start to realize

that those messages ego is feeding
you aren't helpful.
Ego talks over that intuitive voice.
Learning to name
those thoughts as ego is step one

to getting closer to your true nature
and the intuitive voice.

Because once you can 
drown out the voice of ego
that subtle and peaceful and loving
intuitive voice gets a chance to be heard.

Any type of quieting practice
that you can do.

There are all different
forms of meditation.

Mantra meditation,
that's what I like to do.

I use the mantra.
That's not for everyone.

Some people like to do 
meditative prayer.

Breathwork is a form of meditation.
Anything that you can do

that's a quieting practice
to still your heart, to quiet your mind,

that will bring you
so much closer to the light,

so much quicker
than anything else that you can do.

It's a little bit every day
and it will change

as you change
and as you heal and evolve and grow.

Your quieting process
will evolve right along with you.
The more you practice getting quiet,
the easier it is to get there each time.
That's when we can notice
the messages, the synchronicities,
and we can get in the flow of
the natural processes of life.

But if our minds are busy
and if our bodies are busy,
it's very hard to tap into those subtle

but powerful messages
that are always there for us.

That's how we can get closer
to that white light here on Earth
and get closer to our true self,
and what we were put here to do.

A lot of times
people will ask me, If God

or if the Light is so good and so loving,
why is there suffering here?

We won’t fully know the answer to that
until we get to the other side fully.

But the one thing that I do know
is some of our suffering

we can control by stripping away ego
as much as we can,
and getting into that space of love
as often as we can.
Other times, for reasons
that aren't always clear,

there is some suffering
that we just have to endure.
It's painful for me

to say that because I don't like 
to see people
experiencing devastation and suffering.

So what you can make
better, try to do that.

That's when you always pray
or you

do whatever you need to do to get through it or help
the people that you love get through it

and know that with every breath you take,
your life can change.

So when things feel really good now,
soak that up and enjoy it.

And when things get hard,
know that that can change too.
That's where faith is important.
I do believe that we are ultimately meant
to come here and experience abundance
and joy and love.

It's not a punishment
to be here in our bodies and it's okay

to feel happiness
and to have fun.

Part of this Earth game that we're all playing,
it's not meant to be serious all the time.

So look at the bright side too,
when it's there.

Acknowledge everything that's going on,
because simultaneously
there's suffering, there's pain

and there's joy and there's love
and there is humor.
It's all happening at once.

To see the whole picture as much as
we can is part of how we get through it.

Somewhere along the way, 
we started thinking

that there is strength or action
in being in a fearful state.

But really when we're in fear,
we're stuck.

Fear is resistance and fear is glue
and there is no flow there.

It's very hard to find solutions
when you're in a space of fear.
Thinking that fear gives you power,

or means that you're taking action
or paying attention – that's an illusion

because there are very little
possibilities in the space
that fear sits.

So I consistently clear my energy.
Because I know that if I'm stuck

in a low vibrational space,
or if I have something in my nervous system,

or in my emotional space
that I need to process, that I'm hanging on to

for whatever reason – I'm constantly
working to heal and clear that.

Because that lightens
me up enough where I can

more easily focus on the 
positive aspects that are going on.
And gratitude.

Gratitude and self-love,
and self-gratitude – very important.
Because whatever you're focusing on,

that's what you're going to see.
Whatever you're paying attention to,
that's what you're going to see more of.

A gratitude practice 
is really important

first thing when you wake up,
because you are in a state of mind

where you're going to really absorb
whatever's happening.

If the first thing you do is
reach for your phone and check your emails

you're probably going to have
a chaotic day.

If you, when you wake up
in the morning,
can lie in bed and think of five things

that you're grateful for,
that sets the tone for the entire day.

I don't know why it's so hard
for all of us to get into that practice.

But give it a try, because I have noticed
the days that I do that, it's better.

I can see more of the positive
and things just start to happen.

Things just start to flow my way
better than I could have even tried to create for myself.
That's why gratitude is important.

Self-love is incredibly important, too,
because

whatever in your heart
is what spills out to others.

If you're telling yourself
awful things about yourself

or being critical about yourself,
that spills out into the world.

So taking some time
to appreciate yourself and
notice the beauty in yourself.

We are in a dark time in the world
right now.
It's very challenging.

There are a lot of traumatic events
that are taking place

and it feels like we're 
going to be stuck in this place
for a long time,
but I don't see it that way.

I see it as
we need to go through this chaos
in order to hit that reset button.
I see this as, we're coming out of it.
It's just going to take some time.
The more you can go inward

when you feel those triggers
from the outside world,

the more you can heal
and get into a space of love

and get into a space of light
and that ripples outward.

The way that all of us
can make a difference in the world

is just by being yourself,
exactly where you are today.

Do your healing work
and do everything you can
to get back to your true self.

Where you are today is exactly
where you're supposed to be

and you are in your soul purpose,
exactly where you're supposed to be today.

I could look back at my life
five years ago
or before my NDE, and

although I'm
in a much different space now,

I look back and I know
I was in my soul purpose then
because I needed to go through that
to get to where I am today.

And so, offering yourself
the love and acceptance every day,

honoring where you're at 
and continuing to learn,

and continuing to grow, 
and continuing to love.

That's how you can make
a difference in this world.
